question: She has been ____ here since 2020. (A) work (B) works (C) working (D) worked

question: (A) the risks, they decided to ____ (B) with the plan. (A) Despite; (B) proceed / (A) Because; (B) stop / (A) Due to; (B) wait / (A) Given; (B) change

subject: Grammar
question: If the weather is sunny, we will go for a hike. We are going for a hike. Which is the assumption?
hint: The conclusion follows only if the condition was met.
explanation: The argument assumes the condition 'sunny weather' was met because the result occurred.

subject: Grammar
question: Companies that invest in AI see higher profits. TechCorp invests in AI. Therefore, TechCorp will have higher profits. Assumption?
option_d: AI is the only way to profit.
hint: The conclusion relies on the subject fitting the criteria.
explanation: The argument assumes TechCorp qualifies as a company that fits the initial premise.

subject: Grammar
question: People who exercise daily have better health. John exercises daily. Therefore, John has better health. Assumption?
option_b: Exercise is the only way to be healthy.
option_d: John has been exercising for years.
hint: The logic requires the individual to fall into the group mentioned.
explanation: For the conclusion to work, John must be part of the group ('people') that the premise discusses.

subject: Grammar
question: Eating organic food reduces health risks. Apples are organic food. Therefore, eating apples reduces health risks. Assumption?
hint: The logic assumes the specific item is included in the general category.
explanation: The argument assumes apples fall into the organic food category to apply the premise to them.

subject: Grammar
question: Successful businesses have a strong online presence. ABC Store has a strong online presence. So, ABC Store is a successful business. Assumption?
option_c: Online presence is the only factor.
hint: The argument assumes the entity is a member of the set mentioned.
explanation: The argument assumes ABC Store fits the category of 'business' defined in the premise.

subject: Grammar
question: If you sleep 8 hours, you will feel energized. You feel energized. Assumption?
hint: The assumption connects the result back to the specific cause.
explanation: The argument assumes that feeling energized is caused by the 8 hours of sleep mentioned.

subject: Grammar
question: Artists who practice daily improve their skills. Nina practices daily. So, Nina improves her skills. Assumption?
hint: The subject must fit the established group.
explanation: The logic assumes Nina is an 'artist' to apply the premise's rule to her.

subject: Grammar
question: Electric cars reduce carbon emissions. Model X is an electric car. Thus, Model X reduces carbon emissions. Assumption?
hint: The assumption identifies the subject as part of the category.
explanation: The argument requires Model X to be an 'electric car' as defined by the premise.

subject: Grammar
question: Students who read books have a better vocabulary. Ali reads books. So, Ali has a better vocabulary. Assumption?
hint: Connecting the individual to the group.
explanation: The argument assumes Ali is a student to link him to the established fact.

subject: Grammar
question: Most employees in the company are engineers. All engineers are graduates. What can be inferred?
hint: Inferences must be supported by the premises.
explanation: Since most employees are engineers and all engineers are graduates, it follows that at least some employees are graduates.

subject: Grammar
question: Every time it rains, the ground gets wet. Today the ground is not wet. Inference?
hint: Use deductive logic based on the premise.
explanation: If rain always makes the ground wet, and the ground is dry, the rain could not have occurred.

subject: Grammar
question: All fruits are healthy. Some apples are not fruits. Inference?
option_a: Some healthy things are not apples.
explanation: Since some things that are not fruits are healthy, it does not confirm apples are healthy, but supports that healthy things exist outside the fruit category.

subject: Grammar
question: Only experts are invited to the meeting. John is invited to the meeting. Inference?
explanation: The condition 'only experts' means if John is invited, he must be an expert.

subject: Grammar
question: All dogs love bones. My pet loves bones. Inference?
option_a: My pet might be a dog.
hint: Inference must be a possibility or necessity based on facts.
explanation: While my pet could be another animal, the fact that he loves bones is consistent with the behavior of a dog.

subject: Grammar
question: No birds are mammals. Some bats are mammals. Inference?
explanation: Since all mammals (including those specific bats) are not birds, it follows that those bats cannot be birds.

subject: Grammar
question: If the project is delayed, we lose money. We did not lose money. Inference?
explanation: If the delay causes a loss, and there was no loss, then the delay could not have happened.

subject: Grammar
question: All squares are rectangles. Some rectangles are blue. Inference?
explanation: If some rectangles are blue, and some rectangles are squares, it is possible (though not certain) some rectangles are blue.

subject: Grammar
question: Most of the books on the shelf are fiction. Some of the books are biographies. Inference?
option_a: There are at least two types of books.
hint: Analyze the contents of the set.
explanation: Because both fiction and biographies are mentioned, we know the set contains at least two distinct categories.

subject: Grammar
question: Whenever the alarm goes off, there is a fire. The alarm has not gone off. Inference?
option_a: There is not necessarily a fire.
hint: Avoid the fallacy of denying the antecedent.
explanation: The absence of the alarm does not confirm the absence of a fire, it only confirms the condition for the alarm was not met.

subject: Grammar
question: Sales of electric vehicles have increased by 20%. Tax incentives for electric cars were introduced last year. Conclusion?
option_d: Electric cars will replace gas cars.
hint: Identify the most logical outcome supported by the data.
explanation: The correlation between the incentives and the sales growth strongly suggests a causal link.

subject: Grammar
question: Students who use laptops for research perform better on exams. Ali used a laptop for his research. Conclusion?
hint: Conclusion should be a reasonable prediction based on premises.
explanation: Given the positive correlation, it is reasonable to conclude Ali might have a performance advantage.

subject: Grammar
question: Heavy rain caused flooding in the city yesterday. Today, the roads are still blocked. Conclusion?
option_a: The flooding is still affecting travel.
hint: The conclusion links the current state to the past cause.
explanation: The ongoing road blockage is directly attributed to the previous flooding event.

subject: Grammar
question: Eating fruits improves digestion. Rahul eats three fruits daily. Conclusion?
hint: Derive the outcome from the provided rule.
explanation: If fruits aid digestion and Rahul eats them, it follows his digestion should benefit.

subject: Grammar
question: Companies with remote work policies have higher employee retention. Our firm just started a remote work policy. Conclusion?
hint: Predict the result of implementing the policy.
explanation: The premise establishes a pattern that the firm's new policy will likely follow.

subject: Grammar
question: High-intensity exercise leads to muscle gain. Sam does high-intensity exercise. Conclusion?
option_a: Sam is likely to gain muscle.
hint: Apply the general rule to the specific case.
explanation: The conclusion follows directly from the application of the premise to Sam's actions.

subject: Grammar
question: Local farmers provide fresh produce. We buy from local farmers. Conclusion?
explanation: If buying from local farmers means getting fresh produce, then our action leads to that result.

subject: Grammar
question: Investment in education reduces poverty. Nation X increased its education budget. Conclusion?
explanation: Increased budget implies more investment, leading to the outcome mentioned in the premise.

subject: Grammar
question: Early sleepers report higher productivity. Sarah sleeps early. Conclusion?
explanation: Based on the established link, we can conclude Sarah's habit matches the positive outcome.

subject: Grammar
question: Training employees increases morale. The company organized a training session. Conclusion?
explanation: The connection between training and morale leads to the conclusion that the session will have an effect.

subject: Grammar
question: Argument: A new law banning plastic bags will reduce pollution. Which strengthens it?
option_a: Studies show plastic bags make up 40% of city waste.
explanation: By showing that plastic bags are a major component of the waste, the argument for the ban is bolstered.

subject: Grammar
question: Argument: Our coffee shop should offer free Wi-Fi to increase customers. Strengthen?
option_a: Surveys show 80% of locals prefer coffee shops with Wi-Fi.
explanation: Evidence that customers prefer Wi-Fi directly validates the strategy to increase patrons.

subject: Grammar
question: Argument: Implementing a four-day work week increases productivity. Strengthen?
option_a: Data from companies shows a 15% increase in output with this schedule.
explanation: Empirical evidence (data) is the strongest way to support a causal claim about productivity.

subject: Grammar
question: Argument: Increasing the minimum wage will reduce employee turnover. Strengthen?
option_a: Higher pay has been linked to higher job satisfaction in this sector.
explanation: Connecting pay to satisfaction explains why turnover would decrease, making the argument more robust.

subject: Grammar
question: Argument: Solar energy is the best solution for the city's power needs. Strengthen?
option_a: The city has 300 days of sunshine per year.
explanation: Sunlight availability is a prerequisite for solar energy, confirming its suitability for the city.

subject: Grammar
question: Argument: The city should build a new park because it will increase tourism. Weaken?
option_a: Surveys indicate most tourists come for historical monuments, not parks.
option_c: The park is near the center.
hint: Weaken by attacking the premise's impact.
explanation: If tourists aren't interested in parks, the park will fail to increase tourism.

subject: Grammar
question: Argument: Buying a hybrid car saves money on fuel. Weaken?
option_a: The initial cost of a hybrid car is much higher than a gas car.
explanation: If the initial cost outweighs fuel savings, the claim that it 'saves money' is weakened.

subject: Grammar
question: Argument: Using social media for ads is the most effective marketing. Weaken?
option_a: Target audiences for our product do not use social media.
hint: Weaken by showing the strategy is inapplicable.
explanation: If the target market isn't present on the platform, the ads will not be effective.

subject: Grammar
question: Argument: Exercising in the morning is best for weight loss. Weaken?
option_a: Studies show metabolic rates are identical regardless of the time of day.
option_c: People are busy in the morning.
hint: Weaken by refuting the scientific basis.
explanation: If metabolic benefits are the same, the claim that morning is 'best' loses its scientific basis.

subject: Grammar
question: Argument: A ban on sugar in schools will solve obesity. Weaken?
option_a: Students obtain most of their sugar from drinks consumed outside of school.
hint: Weaken by showing the scope is limited.
explanation: If the problem lies outside the school, the school-based solution will not solve the overall problem.

subject: Grammar
question: He said, "I am happy."
option_a: He said that he was happy.
option_b: He said that I was happy.
option_c: He says that he is happy.
option_d: He said that he is happy.
formula: Reporting verb in past tense requires backshift.
explanation: In indirect speech

subject: Grammar
question: She said, "I live in Delhi."
option_a: She said that she lived in Delhi.
option_b: She said that she lives in Delhi.
option_c: She said that she had lived in Delhi.
option_d: She said that she will live in Delhi.
hint: Change simple present to simple past.
explanation: The speaker's present tense statement becomes past in reported speech.

subject: Grammar
question: They said, "We are watching a movie."
option_a: They said that they were watching a movie.
option_b: They said that they are watching a movie.
option_c: They said that we were watching a movie.
option_d: They said that they have been watching.
hint: Change present continuous to past continuous.
explanation: The reporting verb is in the past

subject: Grammar
question: He said, "I have finished my work."
option_a: He said that he had finished his work.
option_b: He said that he has finished his work.
option_c: He said that he finished his work.
option_d: He said that he would finish his work.
hint: Change present perfect to past perfect.
explanation: Present perfect tense shifts to past perfect when the reporting verb is in the past.

subject: Grammar
question: She said, "I will go tomorrow."
option_a: She said that she would go the next day.
option_b: She said that she will go tomorrow.
option_c: She said that she would go tomorrow.
option_d: She said that she should go the next day.
explanation: Time expressions and modals must be adjusted in indirect speech.

subject: Grammar
question: He said, "The sun rises in the east."
option_a: He said that the sun rises in the east.
option_b: He said that the sun rose in the east.
option_c: He said that the sun had risen in the east.
option_d: He said that the sun would rise in the east.
formula: Universal truth stays in present tense.
explanation: Universal truths do not change tense even if the reporting verb is in the past.

subject: Grammar
question: She said, "I can swim."
option_a: She said that she could swim.
option_b: She said that she can swim.
option_c: She said that she could have swum.
option_d: She said that she was able to swim.
explanation: Modals shift to their past forms in reported speech.

subject: Grammar
question: He said, "It is raining."
option_a: He said that it was raining.
option_b: He said that it is raining.
option_c: He said that it has been raining.
option_d: He said that it would be raining.
explanation: Reporting verb in past requires shifting 'is' to 'was'.

subject: Grammar
question: They said, "We were playing."
option_a: They said that they had been playing.
option_b: They said that they were playing.
option_c: They said that we had been playing.
option_d: They said that they have been playing.
hint: Change past continuous to past perfect continuous.
explanation: Past continuous shifts to past perfect continuous in indirect speech.

subject: Grammar
question: She said, "I wrote a letter."
option_a: She said that she had written a letter.
option_b: She said that she wrote a letter.
option_c: She said that she has written a letter.
option_d: She said that she would write a letter.
hint: Change simple past to past perfect.
explanation: Simple past shifts to past perfect in reported speech.

subject: Grammar
question: He asked, "Where are you going?"
option_a: He asked me where I was going.
option_b: He asked me where was I going.
option_c: He asked me where I am going.
option_d: He asked me where am I going.
explanation: In indirect questions

subject: Grammar
question: She asked, "Do you like coffee?"
option_a: She asked if I liked coffee.
option_b: She asked if I like coffee.
option_c: She asked whether did I like coffee.
option_d: She asked did I like coffee.
explanation: Yes/No questions require 'if' or 'whether' and standard statement word order.

subject: Grammar
question: He asked, "Why are you late?"
option_a: He asked why I was late.
option_b: He asked why was I late.
option_c: He asked me why I am late.
option_d: He asked me why was I late.
explanation: The auxiliary 'are' must come after the subject in indirect form.

subject: Grammar
question: She asked, "Can you help me?"
option_a: She asked if I could help her.
option_b: She asked if I can help her.
option_c: She asked could I help her.
option_d: She asked if I could have helped her.
explanation: Modals like 'can' change to 'could' in questions.

subject: Grammar
question: He asked, "Is it ready?"
option_a: He asked if it was ready.
option_b: He asked if it is ready.
option_d: He asked whether is it ready.
explanation: The subject 'it' precedes the verb 'was'.

subject: Grammar
question: She asked, "What is your name?"
option_a: She asked what my name was.
option_b: She asked what was my name.
option_c: She asked what my name is.
option_d: She asked what is my name.
explanation: The verb 'is' changes to 'was' and follows the subject.

subject: Grammar
question: He asked, "Will you come?"
option_a: He asked if I would come.
option_b: He asked if I will come.
option_d: He asked if I would have come.
explanation: Future tense modals shift to their past counterparts.

subject: Grammar
question: She asked, "Where do you live?"
option_b: She asked where did I live.
option_d: She asked where I had lived.
explanation: The simple present 'live' changes to simple past 'lived'.

subject: Grammar
question: He asked, "How is your brother?"
option_a: He asked how my brother was.
option_b: He asked how was my brother.
option_c: He asked how my brother is.
option_d: He asked how is my brother.
explanation: The indirect structure requires the verb to come after the subject.

subject: Grammar
question: She asked, "Have you finished?"
option_a: She asked if I had finished.
option_b: She asked if I have finished.
option_d: She asked whether have I finished.
hint: Shift present perfect to past perfect.
explanation: Perfect tenses move one step back to past perfect.

subject: Grammar
question: The teacher said, "Sit down."
option_a: The teacher ordered me to sit down.
option_b: The teacher said to sit down.
option_c: The teacher ordered that I sit down.
option_d: The teacher requested to sit down.
explanation: Commands use the infinitive form to report the action.

subject: Grammar
question: He said, "Clean your room."
option_a: He told me to clean my room.
option_b: He told me that I clean my room.
option_c: He told that I should clean my room.
option_d: He said me to clean my room.
explanation: Commands are converted using the structure 'told/ordered + to + verb'.

subject: Grammar
question: She said, "Do not run."
option_a: She told me not to run.
option_b: She told me to not run.
option_c: She said that I should not run.
option_d: She told me do not run.
formula: Reporting verb + object + not + to + V1
explanation: For negative commands

subject: Grammar
question: The doctor said, "Take this medicine."
option_a: The doctor advised me to take that medicine.
option_b: The doctor advised that I take this medicine.
option_c: The doctor said to take this medicine.
option_d: The doctor told me I should take this medicine.
explanation: Advise is a more appropriate reporting verb for a doctor's instruction.

subject: Grammar
question: He said, "Go away!"
option_a: He ordered me to go away.
option_b: He said that I go away.
option_c: He said me to go away.
explanation: The imperative mood becomes an infinitive in indirect speech.

subject: Grammar
question: He said, "Please help me."
option_a: He requested me to help him.
option_b: He requested that I help him.
option_c: He asked me to please help him.
option_d: He said to me to help him.
explanation: The word 'please' is omitted and replaced by the verb 'requested'.

subject: Grammar
question: She said, "Kindly wait here."
option_a: She requested me to wait there.
option_b: She requested that I wait here.
option_c: She requested me to kindly wait there.
explanation: Spatial markers like 'here' change to 'there' in reported speech.

subject: Grammar
question: He said, "Could you lend me a pen?"
option_a: He requested me to lend him a pen.
option_b: He requested me if I could lend him a pen.
option_c: He asked me to lend him a pen.
option_d: He said to lend him a pen.
explanation: Polite requests are converted to indirect requests using the infinitive.

subject: Grammar
question: She said, "Would you mind opening the window?"
option_a: She requested me to open the window.
option_b: She requested if I would mind opening the window.
option_c: She asked me to please open the window.
option_d: She said that I should open the window.
explanation: Complex requests are simplified to standard indirect request form.

subject: Grammar
question: He said, "Please don't go."
option_a: He requested me not to go.
option_b: He requested me to not go.
option_c: He requested that I should not go.
option_d: He said me not to go.
explanation: The negative infinitive 'not to' is the correct structure.

subject: Grammar
question: He said, "I have been working hard. I need a break."
option_a: He said that he had been working hard and that he needed a break.
option_b: He said that he has been working hard and that he needs a break.
option_c: He said that he had been working hard and that he needs a break.
option_d: He said he had been working hard and he needed a break.
formula: Present Perfect Cont -> Past Perfect Cont
explanation: Both sentences are in the past reporting context and must shift accordingly.

subject: Grammar
question: She said, "Will you stay here? I have much to tell you."
option_a: She asked if I would stay there and said that she had much to tell me.
option_b: She asked if I will stay here and said she has much to tell me.
option_c: She asked if I would stay here and said she had much to tell me.
option_d: She asked if I would stay there and said she has much to tell me.

subject: Grammar
question: He said, "Don't worry, I will handle it."

option_c: He said that I should not worry and he would handle it.

subject: Grammar
question: She said, "I don't like tea. Do you like coffee?"

option_c: She said she doesn't like tea and asked if I like coffee.
option_d: She said that she didn't like tea and asked if I like coffee.
explanation: Tense changes apply to both the statement and the question.

subject: Grammar
question: He said, "I am leaving now. Can you come later?"

option_d: He said he was leaving now and asked could I come later.

subject: Grammar
question: She said, "It is a nice day. Why don't we go out?"

option_d: She said it was a nice day and asked why we didn't go out.

subject: Verbal Ability

(D) Therefore, international agreements like the Paris Accord are necessary. (E) Without a unified approach, individual efforts will fall short.
explanation: A is the topic, B is the elaboration, C is the example, D is the logical consequence, and E is the reinforced conclusion.

subject: Verbal Ability
question: (A) The new policy was expected to streamline production. (B) However, the implementation phase faced unexpected technical hurdles. (C) Employees struggled to adapt to the new software. (D) Consequently, the company decided to delay the rollout by two months. (E) During this period, additional training sessions were conducted.
hint: Identify the transition from expectation to complication.
explanation: A is the intent, B is the contrast, C is the detail of the contrast, D is the result, and E is the subsequent action.

subject: Verbal Ability
question: (A) Education has traditionally been classroom-based. (B) Conversely, the digital age has shifted learning to online platforms. (C) While this offers flexibility, it also lacks personal mentorship. (D) To bridge this, hybrid models are gaining traction. (E) This approach combines the benefits of both traditional and digital systems.
explanation: A is the past, B is the contrast, C is the critical analysis, D is the proposal, and E is the definition of the proposal.

subject: Verbal Ability
question: (A) Global trade has boosted economic prosperity in many nations. (B) It has allowed for the exchange of goods and expertise. (C) However, it has also led to increased dependence on international supply chains. (D) Any disruption in one part of the world now has a ripple effect. (E) Thus, countries are now focusing on regional self-sufficiency.
hint: Identify the shift in global trade dynamics.
explanation: A is the benefit, B is the detail, C is the twist, D is the consequence of the twist, and E is the logical adaptation.

subject: Grammar
question: Select the adjective in the sentence: "The bright sun warmed the cold ground."
explanation: Bright describes the noun 'sun'.

subject: Grammar
question: Identify the preposition in the following sentence: "She sat beside the river for hours."
hint: Prepositions show relationship in space or time.
explanation: Beside indicates the location of the subject relative to the river.

subject: Grammar

subject: Verbal Ability
question: Passage: Regular exercise is not merely about physical appearance; it is a fundamental pillar of mental well-being. Studies have shown that aerobic activity releases endorphins, the body's natural mood lifters. Even a brief twenty-minute walk can significantly reduce anxiety and improve cognitive focus throughout the workday. Question: According to the passage, what is the primary benefit of exercise mentioned?
hint: The text emphasizes mental well-being over physical appearance.
explanation: The passage explicitly states exercise is a 'fundamental pillar of mental well-being' and leads to reduced anxiety.

subject: Verbal Ability
question: Passage: The ancient art of storytelling has evolved significantly with the advent of digital media. While oral traditions relied on memory and face-to-face interaction, modern platforms allow stories to reach global audiences instantly. Despite this, the core purpose of storytelling—to evoke emotion and share wisdom—remains unchanged. Question: What remains constant in storytelling according to the author?
option_c: (C) The core purpose of the act
hint: The passage mentions that despite evolution, some things do not change.
explanation: The passage explicitly states 'the core purpose of storytelling... remains unchanged' despite the evolution in medium.

subject: Verbal Ability
question: Passage: Renewable energy sources like wind and solar power are becoming more affordable than traditional fossil fuels. This economic shift is driving countries to rethink their power grids. By investing in sustainable infrastructure, nations can reduce carbon emissions and ensure long-term energy security. Question: What is the main reason countries are rethinking their power grids?
option_b: (B) The high cost of solar power
option_c: (C) The economic advantage of renewable energy
hint: Identify the cause-and-effect relationship in the second sentence.
explanation: The passage states that the 'economic shift' (renewables becoming more affordable) is the driver for rethinking power grids.

subject: Verbal Ability
question: Passage: Honeybees are essential to our agricultural systems because they pollinate a vast array of crops. Recent studies, however, indicate a alarming decline in bee populations due to habitat loss and pesticide use. If this trend continues, global food security may be at risk. Question: What poses a threat to global food security according to the text?
hint: Locate the consequence of the declining trend.
explanation: The passage links the decline of bee populations to a potential risk for global food security.

subject: Verbal Ability
question: Passage: Time management is not about working longer hours, but about working smarter. By prioritizing high-impact tasks and eliminating distractions, one can achieve more in less time. Those who master this skill often find more balance between their professional and personal lives. Question: How does the author define effective time management?
hint: The first sentence provides the author's definition.
explanation: The author defines it as 'working smarter' and 'prioritizing high-impact tasks'.

subject: Verbal Ability
question: Passage: The local farmer's market has seen a surge in popularity this season. Shoppers appreciate the freshness of the produce, which is harvested just hours before being sold. This direct-to-consumer approach supports local families and reduces the carbon footprint associated with long-distance transportation. Question: What is an advantage of the farmer's market mentioned?
hint: Identify the benefits of the market mentioned in the text.
explanation: The passage mentions the approach 'supports local families' as a key advantage.

subject: Verbal Ability
question: Passage: Music education has been shown to improve mathematical skills in young children. By learning to count beats and understand patterns, the brain develops stronger analytical pathways. It seems that melody and logic are more closely linked than many realize. Question: How does music education influence the brain?
hint: Look for the phrase regarding brain development.
explanation: The passage states that learning music helps the brain develop 'stronger analytical pathways'.

subject: Verbal Ability
question: Passage: Remote work has provided employees with unprecedented flexibility. However, it requires a high degree of self-discipline to remain productive. Without a traditional office environment, workers must set clear boundaries between their work hours and their personal time. Question: What does remote work require from an employee?
hint: Look for the requirement mentioned regarding productivity.
explanation: The passage explicitly mentions that remote work 'requires a high degree of self-discipline'.

subject: Verbal Ability
question: Passage: The book fair was a massive success, attracting thousands of enthusiasts. Authors held signing sessions, and panel discussions offered insights into various genres. The organizers are already planning for an even larger event next year. Question: What is the outcome of the book fair?
hint: The first sentence summarizes the outcome.
explanation: The passage states the fair was a 'massive success'.

subject: Verbal Ability
question: Passage: Water is a precious resource that is often taken for granted. While the earth is covered in water, most of it is saline and unsuitable for drinking. Conservation efforts are crucial to ensure that future generations have access to clean, fresh water. Question: Why is conservation of water important according to the passage?
option_a: (A) Most of the earth's water is saline
option_b: (B) We have too much fresh water
option_c: (C) Water is not a precious resource
hint: Identify the reason given for the need to conserve.
explanation: The passage explains that most of the earth's water is 'saline and unsuitable for drinking,' necessitating conservation.

subject: Verbal Ability
question: Passage: Gardening is a therapeutic activity for many. It involves patience, as plants require time to grow and flourish. The act of nurturing a plant from a seed to a flower provides a sense of accomplishment. Question: Why is gardening considered therapeutic?
option_c: (C) It provides a sense of accomplishment
hint: Look for the emotional benefit mentioned.
explanation: The passage states that the act of nurturing a plant 'provides a sense of accomplishment'.

subject: Verbal Ability
question: Passage: In the digital age, cybersecurity is a priority for every business. Data breaches can lead to financial losses and loss of customer trust. Implementing strong security protocols is the only way to safeguard sensitive company information. Question: What is the consequence of a data breach?
hint: Look for the impact of a data breach.
explanation: The text states data breaches lead to 'financial losses and loss of customer trust'.

subject: Verbal Ability
question: Passage: Walking is the most underrated form of exercise. It is accessible, requires no equipment, and can be done anywhere. Incorporating a daily walk into your routine can improve your heart health and mood significantly. Question: What is the advantage of walking as an exercise?
option_c: (C) It is accessible and requires no equipment
option_d: (D) It is not good for the heart
explanation: The passage highlights that walking is 'accessible' and 'requires no equipment'.

subject: Verbal Ability
question: Passage: The museum's new exhibit features artifacts from the Bronze Age. Visitors can see tools, jewelry, and pottery that reveal much about the daily lives of ancient people. It is a rare opportunity to step back in time. Question: What does the exhibit provide to visitors?
hint: Identify the purpose of the artifacts.
explanation: The passage notes that the artifacts 'reveal much about the daily lives of ancient people'.

subject: Verbal Ability
question: Passage: Reading daily helps expand vocabulary and improves communication skills. By engaging with different perspectives, a reader gains a better understanding of the world. It is a lifelong habit that benefits the mind. Question: What is a long-term benefit of reading?
explanation: The passage lists 'expand vocabulary' and 'improves communication' as benefits.

subject: Verbal Ability
question: Passage: The festival of lights is celebrated with great fervor across the country. Families decorate their homes, exchange gifts, and enjoy traditional sweets. It is a time for unity and spreading joy. Question: How is the festival celebrated?
option_b: (B) By decorating homes and exchanging gifts
explanation: The passage describes decorating homes, exchanging gifts, and enjoying sweets.

subject: Verbal Ability
question: Passage: Sleep is essential for the body to recover and rejuvenate. A lack of sleep can lead to decreased concentration and irritability. Health experts recommend at least seven hours of sleep per night for adults. Question: What is the consequence of sleep deprivation?
hint: Look for the negative effects mentioned.
explanation: The passage states lack of sleep leads to 'decreased concentration'.

subject: Verbal Ability
question: Passage: The public park offers a sanctuary for city dwellers. With its lush trees and quiet walking paths, it provides a break from the noise and concrete. On weekends, it is a hub of community activity. Question: How is the park described?
option_c: (C) As a sanctuary for city dwellers
hint: Identify the metaphor used for the park.
explanation: The park is explicitly called a 'sanctuary for city dwellers'.

subject: Verbal Ability
question: Passage: Learning a musical instrument teaches discipline. It takes hours of practice to master even a simple tune. The journey from a novice to a player is filled with small victories and constant learning. Question: What does learning a musical instrument teach?
explanation: The passage explicitly states that learning an instrument 'teaches discipline'.

subject: Verbal Ability
question: Passage: The rapid advancement of artificial intelligence (AI) has sparked a global debate regarding its long-term impact on the workforce. While proponents argue that AI will automate mundane tasks and create new job categories, critics fear that it will lead to widespread displacement of workers in sectors like manufacturing and customer service. Furthermore, the ethical implications of AI-driven decision-making, such as bias in algorithms, remain a significant concern for policymakers. To navigate this transformation, there is a growing consensus that education systems must prioritize critical thinking and adaptability over rote memorization. Question: According to the passage, what is the primary concern regarding AI in the workforce?
option_a: (A) It will make work too easy
option_b: (B) It will eliminate all jobs immediately
option_c: (C) The potential displacement of human workers
hint: Synthesize the argument regarding the workforce.
explanation: The passage notes that critics fear 'widespread displacement of workers' due to AI.

subject: Verbal Ability
question: Passage: The urbanization of the global population is an irreversible trend. As more people move to cities in search of better opportunities, the strain on urban infrastructure becomes increasingly evident. Waste management, traffic congestion, and air quality are becoming critical issues that city planners must address. Innovative solutions such as smart traffic systems and green urban design are being tested in several metropolitan areas. However, for these solutions to be effective, there must be a coordinated effort between government authorities, private enterprises, and the citizens themselves. Question: What is necessary for the success of smart urban solutions according to the passage?
option_b: (B) A coordinated effort between all stakeholders
option_d: (D) Reducing the number of city planners
hint: Look for the conditions for effectiveness.
explanation: The passage states that for solutions to be effective, there must be a 'coordinated effort between government authorities, private enterprises, and the citizens'.

subject: Verbal Ability
question: Passage: In the field of medicine, the concept of personalized therapy is revolutionizing patient care. By analyzing an individual's genetic profile, doctors can tailor treatments that are more effective and have fewer side effects compared to standardized protocols. This precision medicine approach has already shown promising results in treating chronic diseases like diabetes and cancer. Despite the potential, the high cost of genetic testing remains a barrier to widespread adoption in developing nations. Future efforts must focus on democratizing this technology to ensure equitable health outcomes for all populations. Question: What is the main barrier to the widespread adoption of personalized therapy?
option_b: (B) The high cost of genetic testing
hint: Identify the challenge mentioned in the text.
explanation: The passage explicitly mentions 'the high cost of genetic testing' as a barrier to adoption.

subject: Verbal Ability
question: Passage: The history of the Silk Road is a testament to the power of global connectivity. It was not merely a path for trading silk, but a vibrant highway for the exchange of religions, philosophies, and technologies between the East and the West. This intercultural dialogue profoundly shaped the civilizations of Eurasia. Even today, the legacy of this ancient network reminds us that isolated development is rare and that progress is often the result of cross-border collaboration. Question: What was the primary significance of the Silk Road beyond trade?
option_a: (A) It was the only way to travel
option_b: (B) It facilitated the exchange of culture and ideas
option_c: (C) It led to the isolation of civilizations
option_d: (D) It was limited to military use
explanation: The passage states it was a 'vibrant highway for the exchange of religions, philosophies, and technologies'.

subject: Verbal Ability
question: Passage: While global tourism provides significant economic benefits to local communities, its environmental impact cannot be ignored. The influx of tourists often leads to increased waste production, strain on local resources, and the degradation of natural habitats. Sustainable tourism has emerged as a response to these challenges, emphasizing the need for smaller-scale, responsible travel that respects the local environment. By choosing eco-friendly accommodations and respecting local wildlife, travelers can help mitigate their impact and ensure that these destinations remain preserved for future generations. Question: What is the core principle of sustainable tourism mentioned?
option_b: (B) Prioritizing the economy over the environment
option_c: (C) Responsible travel that minimizes environmental impact
hint: Define the concept of sustainable tourism based on the text.
explanation: Sustainable tourism is defined by the need for 'smaller-scale, responsible travel' that minimizes damage.

subject: Verbal Ability
question: Passage: The philosophy of Stoicism teaches that we cannot control external events, but we can control our reactions to them. This perspective is particularly useful in the high-stress environment of modern corporate life. By practicing mindfulness and maintaining perspective, professionals can remain calm amidst chaos. Stoicism does not advocate for indifference, but rather for a rational approach to challenges. It encourages individuals to focus their energy on what is within their power and to let go of what is not. Question: What is the core lesson of Stoicism according to the passage?
option_c: (C) To focus on personal reactions rather than external outcomes
explanation: The passage summarizes the philosophy as focusing on what we can control—our reactions—rather than external events.

subject: Verbal Ability
question: Passage: The rapid melting of polar ice caps has become a visible indicator of global warming. Beyond the loss of habitat for Arctic species, this process contributes to rising sea levels, posing a threat to coastal communities worldwide. Scientists emphasize that there is a limited window to implement carbon-reduction strategies before the damage becomes irreversible. International cooperation is essential, as this is a collective challenge that transcends national borders. The transition to a green economy is not just an environmental imperative but a necessary step for long-term global stability. Question: What is the broader consequence of the melting ice caps mentioned in the text?
option_b: (B) Rising sea levels threatening coastal areas
hint: Look for the specific consequence listed.
explanation: The passage explicitly mentions that melting ice 'contributes to rising sea levels, posing a threat to coastal communities'.

subject: Verbal Ability
question: Passage: Mentorship is often cited as a critical factor in career success. A good mentor provides not just technical guidance, but also emotional support and networking opportunities. It is a reciprocal relationship where both parties benefit; the mentor gains fresh perspectives, while the mentee gains wisdom and experience. For mentorship to be truly effective, it must be built on a foundation of trust and open communication. It is not about telling someone what to do, but helping them find their own path. Question: What makes a mentorship relationship 'reciprocal'?
option_a: (A) It is only about the mentor's benefit
option_c: (C) Both the mentor and mentee gain benefits
explanation: The passage states it is 'reciprocal' because 'both parties benefit'.

subject: Verbal Ability
question: Passage: The rise of e-learning has democratized education, making high-quality courses accessible to anyone with an internet connection. This has been particularly beneficial for working professionals who need to upskill without leaving their jobs. However, the lack of face-to-face interaction can sometimes lead to feelings of isolation and a decrease in engagement. To address this, many platforms are integrating live discussion forums and interactive projects. The future of education seems to be a blend of digital convenience and human connection. Question: What is a disadvantage of e-learning mentioned?
option_b: (B) It does not provide high-quality courses
explanation: The passage mentions 'lack of face-to-face interaction' can lead to 'feelings of isolation'.

subject: Verbal Ability
question: Passage: The tradition of tea ceremonies in various cultures represents more than just a beverage; it is a ritual of mindfulness and respect. Whether it is the intricate movements of the Japanese ceremony or the social bonding of the British afternoon tea, these traditions serve as an anchor in a fast-paced world. By setting aside time for this ritual, individuals are encouraged to pause, reflect, and appreciate the present moment. It is a simple practice that fosters emotional balance. Question: How are tea traditions described in the text?
option_b: (B) As rituals of mindfulness and respect
option_c: (C) As a way to avoid work
hint: Summarize the description of the ceremonies.
explanation: The passage describes them as 'a ritual of mindfulness and respect'.

subject: Verbal Ability
question: Passage: The architecture of the modern smart city integrates technology into every aspect of life. From automated energy grids to public transport systems controlled by artificial intelligence, these cities aim to improve efficiency and reduce environmental impact. Despite these benefits, critics argue that the reliance on technology creates vulnerabilities in the infrastructure. Cyberattacks on a city-wide scale could have devastating consequences. Thus, security is a paramount concern for engineers designing these futuristic environments. Question: What is the main concern about smart cities according to the passage?
hint: Identify the concern raised by critics.
explanation: The passage notes that critics worry about 'vulnerabilities' resulting in 'cyberattacks'.

subject: Verbal Ability
question: Passage: The importance of gut health has recently gained mainstream attention. A balanced microbiome is essential for digestion, immune function, and even emotional regulation. Recent research shows that the gut-brain axis is a two-way communication system. A diet rich in fiber and fermented foods can foster a healthy gut, which in turn benefits overall health. Understanding this connection allows people to take a more holistic approach to their daily diet and well-being. Question: What is the relationship between the gut and the brain?
option_b: (B) They communicate through a two-way system
option_c: (C) The brain has no effect on the gut
option_d: (D) Only the gut influences the brain
explanation: The passage states the gut-brain axis is a 'two-way communication system'.

subject: Verbal Ability
question: Passage: The creative process is often messy and non-linear. Many people make the mistake of waiting for inspiration to strike, but professionals know that creativity is a discipline. By setting a regular schedule and pushing through periods of doubt, one eventually finds the breakthrough. It is not about genius; it is about persistence. Those who succeed are the ones who treat creativity as a job rather than a hobby. Question: How does the author describe the creative process?
option_a: (A) As a linear and easy process
option_d: (C) As something that only geniuses have
correct_option: (D) As a hobby that should be done whenever
formula: Analyze the description of the process.
explanation: Descriptive comprehension. The author describes it as 'messy and non-linear' and 'a discipline'.

subject: Verbal Ability
question: Passage: The decline of traditional craftsmanship is a concern for those who value heritage. As mass-produced items become more affordable and accessible, the demand for handcrafted goods has dwindled. However, there is a small but growing movement of consumers who prioritize quality, uniqueness, and the story behind an object. These consumers are willing to pay a premium for items made by master artisans. This shift suggests a potential future where craftsmanship is valued as a luxury rather than a daily necessity. Question: Why is the decline of craftsmanship a concern?
option_a: (A) Because mass-produced items are not good
option_b: (B) Because it represents a loss of heritage
option_c: (C) Because it is impossible to make things by hand
explanation: The passage links the concern to those 'who value heritage'.

subject: Verbal Ability
question: Passage: The concept of a circular economy aims to minimize waste by reusing and recycling materials. Unlike the traditional 'take-make-dispose' model, this system focuses on keeping products in use for as long as possible. Many industries are now redesigning their supply chains to incorporate circular principles. While the transition requires significant investment, the long-term environmental and economic gains are substantial. It is a shift from ownership to access, where the focus is on the performance of a product rather than its possession. Question: What is the core goal of a circular economy?
option_b: (B) To minimize waste through reuse and recycling
option_d: (D) To focus only on owning products
hint: Look for the definition of circular economy.
explanation: The passage defines it as aiming to 'minimize waste by reusing and recycling materials'.

subject: Verbal Ability
question: Passage: The development of space-based solar power could provide the world with near-infinite clean energy. By placing solar panels in orbit, energy can be collected without the interference of weather or night cycles. The challenge lies in the engineering required to beam this power back to earth safely and efficiently. While currently theoretical, several private and public agencies are actively researching this technology. If successful, it could fundamentally alter the global energy landscape. Question: What is the main benefit of space-based solar power?
option_a: (A) It is cheaper than earth-based solar
option_b: (B) It provides energy without weather or night interruptions
option_d: (D) It is already in use globally
hint: Identify the unique benefit of space-based power.
explanation: The passage notes it can be collected 'without the interference of weather or night cycles'.

subject: Verbal Ability
question: Passage: Diversity in the workplace is not just an ethical imperative; it is a business strategy. Studies have consistently shown that teams with diverse backgrounds bring a wider range of perspectives, leading to better problem-solving and innovation. When employees feel included and valued, their engagement and productivity increase. Organizations that fail to embrace diversity often suffer from groupthink and a lack of adaptability in a changing market. Thus, promoting inclusivity is essential for long-term competitiveness. Question: What is the business advantage of a diverse workplace?
option_c: (C) It leads to better innovation and problem-solving
option_d: (D) It is only an ethical decision
hint: Look for the business benefits mentioned.

subject: Verbal Ability
question: Passage: The importance of sleep hygiene cannot be overstated. This adaptation might ensure their survival but could also lead to changes in their mating behaviors, as song is a key part of their courtship. Question: What can be inferred about the bird's adaptation?

subject: Verbal Ability

subject: Verbal Ability

subject: Verbal Ability

subject: Verbal Ability
question: The reason for his failure is because he did not study.
option_a: The reason for his failure is that he did not study
option_b: The reason for his failure is due to not studying
option_c: The reason for his failure is because of not studying
option_d: The reason for his failure is that he didn't study
explanation: 'The reason... is that' is the grammatically correct structure.

subject: Verbal Ability
question: Walking down the street, the trees looked beautiful.
option_a: While I was walking down the street
option_d: I found the trees looked beautiful
explanation: the trees look beautiful A The subject of the main clause must perform the participle action. Dangling Modifiers 'Walking down the street' describes a person, not the trees.

subject: Verbal Ability
question: The book, which I borrowed from the library, is very interesting.
option_a: The book that I borrowed from the library is very interesting
option_b: The book which I borrowed it from the library is very interesting
option_c: The book I borrowed from the library
hint: that I borrowed from the library
explanation: A Restrictive vs. non-restrictive clauses. Relative Pronouns 'That' is preferred for restrictive clauses; 'which' often requires commas.

subject: Verbal Ability
question: He asked me where did I live.
option_a: He asked me where I lived
option_b: He asked me where I do live
option_c: He asked me where live I
option_d: He asked me where had I lived
hint: Indirect questions do not use inversion.
explanation: 'Asked' introduces an indirect question, which follows subject-verb order.

subject: Verbal Ability
question: Despite the rain, we went for a walk.
explanation: we went for a walk A Despite does not take 'of'. Preposition Usage 'Despite' is a preposition equivalent to 'in spite of'.

subject: Verbal Ability
question: None of the candidates has shown interest in the new policy.
option_a: None of the candidates have shown interest in the new policy
option_b: None of the candidates has shown interest in the new policy
option_c: No one of the candidates have shown interest in the new policy
option_d: None of the candidates is showing interest in the new policy
explanation: 'None' can take either singular or plural; however, in formal contexts with a plural prepositional object, plural is common.

subject: Verbal Ability
question: The jury is divided in their opinions.
option_a: The jury are divided in their opinions
option_b: The jury is divided in its opinions
option_c: The jury are divided in its opinions
option_d: The jury is divided in their opinion
explanation: When members of a group are acting individually ('divided'), use the plural verb.

subject: Verbal Ability
question: Each of the participants were asked to provide their feedback.
option_a: Each of the participants was asked to provide his or her feedback
option_b: Each of the participants were asked to provide his feedback
option_c: Each of the participants was asked to provide their feedback
option_d: Each of the participants were asked to provide their feedbacks
explanation: 'Each' is singular, so it requires a singular verb ('was') and singular pronoun.

subject: Verbal Ability
question: It is high time you start studying for the exam.
option_a: It is high time you started studying for the exam
option_b: It is high time you have started studying for the exam
option_c: It is high time you are starting studying for the exam
option_d: It is high time you will start studying for the exam
hint: The phrase 'it is high time' takes the past tense.
explanation: The expression 'it is high time' requires the past subjunctive (or past simple) to indicate an urgent action.

subject: Verbal Ability
question: He behaves as if he was the owner of the house.
option_a: He behaves as if he were the owner of the house
option_b: He behaves as if he is the owner of the house
option_c: He behaves as if he had been the owner of the house
option_d: He behaves as if he would be the owner of the house
explanation: 'As if' with a counter-factual condition requires 'were'.

subject: Verbal Ability
question: Little did I know that he was lying to me.
option_a: Little I knew that he was lying to me
option_b: Little did I knew that he was lying to me
option_c: Little do I know that he was lying to me
option_d: Little did I know that he has been lying to me
explanation: Inversion (did + subject + base verb) is required when starting with 'Little'.

subject: Verbal Ability
question: Not only he completed the work, but he also submitted it on time.
option_a: Not only did he complete the work
option_b: but he also submitted it on time
option_c: Not only he completed the work
option_d: but also submitted it on time
correct_option: Not only did he complete the work
hint: but also he submitted it on time
formula: Not only he did complete the work
explanation: but he also submitted it on time A Inversion in 'not only' constructions. Inversion When starting a sentence with 'not only', inversion ('did he complete') is required.

subject: Verbal Ability
question: Only after the investigation was complete did the truth emerge.
option_a: Only after the investigation was complete the truth emerged
option_b: Only after the investigation was complete
option_d: Only after the investigation was complete did the truth emerged
correct_option: Only after the investigation was complete the truth has emerged
explanation: Inversion Fronting with 'Only after' triggers auxiliary inversion in the main clause.

subject: Verbal Ability
question: More than one student has failed the test.
option_a: More than one student have failed the test
option_b: More than one students have failed the test
option_c: More than one students has failed the test
option_d: More than one student have failed the tests
explanation: 'More than one' takes a singular noun and a singular verb.

subject: Verbal Ability
question: I would rather you left now.
option_a: I would rather you leave now
option_b: I would rather you to leave now
option_c: I would rather you have left now
option_d: I would rather you will leave now
explanation: 'Would rather' + subject + past tense indicates a preference for someone else's action.
